serving.description: 1 medium leek (white and light green parts)
serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw leek (white and light green parts only, approximately 89 g) is a typical single serving used in cooking or salads.

nutrient_source.title: Leek, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for raw leek (F14010026). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients not reported by HPB; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11246 (Leeks, (bulb and lower leaf-portion), raw) for saturated fat and additional micronutrients where HPB data is absent. USDA SR Legacy reports saturated fat 0.04 g/100g, vitamin C 12.0 mg/100g, vitamin A 83 mcg RAE/100g, folate 64 mcg DFE/100g, magnesium 28 mg/100g, phosphorus 35 mg/100g, zinc 0.12 mg/100g, water 83.0 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw leek is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with other raw allium vegetables (onion GI ~10–15, spring onion GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 14.15 × 0.89) / 100 ≈ 1.9 g per 89 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (20 mg/100g) and meaningful potassium content (180 mg/100g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw leek is suitable for hypertensive individuals following the DASH dietary pattern; its low sodium and moderate potassium content support blood pressure management, provided it is not prepared with high-sodium sauces or seasonings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated glycemic index (~15), low glycemic load per serving (~1.9 g), and moderate dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw leek is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its low GI and low glycemic load contribute to blunted postprandial glycaemia, consistent with ADA dietary guidance favouring non-starchy vegetables, though it should not be paired with high-GI refined carbohydrate dishes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and presence of flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw leek is cardioprotective in the context of a heart-healthy diet; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, and flavonoid content (quercetin, kaempferol) are consistent with AHA dietary recommendations for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Flavonoid content (quercetin, kaempferol) and vitamin C providing antioxidant support; however, raw alliums may cause digestive sensitivity in some RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw leek may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as part of an anti-inflammatory diet; those with digestive sensitivity to raw alliums should consider lightly cooking the leek to improve tolerance without significantly reducing its nutritional value.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Leek (raw) is a mild-flavoured allium vegetable (Allium ampeloprasum var. porrum) characterised by a white cylindrical bulb base, pale green lower stalk, and darker green outer leaves. Raw leek has a firm, crisp texture and a flavour milder and sweeter than onion, with subtle grassy and slightly peppery notes. It is widely used in European, Middle Eastern, and global cuisines as a base aromatic in soups, stews, quiches, and salads. Per 100 g, raw leek provides 61 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 14.15 g carbohydrate, 3.9 g sugar, 1.8 g dietary fibre, 20 mg sodium, 180 mg potassium, 59 mg calcium, and 2.1 mg iron. Its low estimated glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load make it suitable for blood glucose management. The negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol content support cardiovascular health. Leek contains flavonoids including quercetin and kaempferol, which have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, raw leek is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11246 (supplementary micronutrients).

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary minerals sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14010026, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and additional micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11246 for raw leek, an exact food match. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed data for raw allium vegetables; this is the only limitation preventing a fully exact GI match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.004 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.167 g/100g), trans fat (0.0 g/100g), and select micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11246 (Leeks, bulb and lower leaf-portion, raw) as HPB Foodo does not report these fields for this entry. 2. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100g) per HPB source; leek is a plant food and inherently contains no cholesterol. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mg/100g and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100g — both are naturally zero as leek is a plant food with no animal-derived components. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on raw allium vegetables (onion, spring onion) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for raw leek was found in approved sources. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/absent in raw plant foods per USDA SR Legacy. 6. Food subgroup updated from 'Leafy vegetables' to 'Allium vegetables' to reflect botanical classification; leek is an allium, not a leafy vegetable.
